turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: All right. Second case, Harrington versus Purdue Pharma. Between 1999 and 2019, approximately a quarter of a million people in the United States died from prescription opioid overdoses. The Federal Government estimates that the opioid epidemic has cost this country between $53 and $72 billion annually. This case involves Purdue Pharma and the Sackler family. For years, Purdue aggressively marketed OxyContin, an opioid pain reliever. The company claimed its drug was less addictive and less subject to abuse than other medications. The Sackler family owned and controlled Purdue and oversaw the firm’s marketing strategies. Eventually, those strategies came under scrutiny. In 2007, a Purdue affiliate pleaded guilty to a federal felony for misleadingly marketing OxyContin as less addictive than it really was. That guilty plea triggered thousands of civil lawsuits as individuals, families, and governments sought damages from both Purdue and the Sacklers for injuries allegedly caused by their deceptive marketing practices. In response to this litigation, the Sacklers divide what they described as a milking program to drain Purdue of its assets and divert its funds overseas to trusts and other entities owned by the family. Between 2008 and 2016, the Sacklers distributed approximately $11 billion of Purdue’s revenue to themselves, leaving the company in a significantly weakened financial state, and in 2019, the company filed for bankruptcy. In Purdue’s bankruptcy, the Sacklers saw, as they put it, an opportunity to get their own goals accomplished. Specifically, they proposed to return to Purdue’s bankruptcy estate around 4.3 of the $11 billion they had withdrawn. But they agreed to do so only if Purdue would include in its bankruptcy plan a special provision for them, one that would extinguish forever any suits against members of the Sackler family by opioid victims, even claims for wrongful death and fraud, and all without the consent of the victims themselves. Purdue agreed to include this provision in its bankruptcy plan, and ultimately a divided panel of the Second Circuit approved that move. The U.S. trustee responsible for overseeing the administration of federal bankruptcies asked us to review the decision. He stressed that there exists a longstanding split among Federal Courts of Appeals over whether bankruptcy courts enjoy the power to extinguish claims by non-debtors like the victims against other non-debtors like the Sacklers. We agreed to take up the case to resolve that split of authority, and in brief, here is what we decide. Under Federal Law, bankruptcy courts have many and extensive powers when it comes to adjusting the rights and duties of those in bankruptcy. When a debtor comes forward and puts all of its assets on the table for its creditors, a bankruptcy court may extinguish many existing claims against the debtor, often in order to allow the debtor to make a fresh start. Here that means the Court was free to extinguish claims against Purdue itself. At the same time, however, the Bankruptcy Code’s focus is squarely on the debtor. Nothing in it permits a court to wipe the slate clean for others outside the bankruptcy, like the Sacklers, who have not come forward with all of their assets for their creditors. Nor does the Bankruptcy Code permit a court, even when it comes to debtors in bankruptcy, to extinguish claims for wrongful death and fraud as the Sacklers seek here. Put another way, in this case the Sacklers ask for more relief than a bankruptcy court may provide even a debtor who actually declares bankruptcy and the Sacklers seek that relief, all while putting on the table far less than their full assets as a debtor in bankruptcy must. In short, we agree with U.S. trustee. The Bankruptcy Code focuses on the debtors’ rights and liabilities. Nothing in it permits a court to extinguish claims against non-debtors like the Sacklers that belong to other non-debtors like the victims, all without their consent. Because the Second Circuit held otherwise, its judgment is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice, Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan have joined.
